elements.sent-2411-001: An inter-combination transition in Yb enables a novel approach for rapidly imaging magnetic field variations with excellent spatial and temporal resolution and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-002: This quantum imaging magnetometer reveals ``dark stripes" that are contours of constant magnetic field visible by eye or capturable by standard cameras.
elements.sent-2411-003: These dark lines result from a combination of Autler-Townes splitting and the spatial Hanle effect in the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition of Yb when driven by multiple strong coherent laser fields (carrier and AM/FM modulation sidebands of a single-mode 556 nm laser).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: We show good agreement between experimental data and our theoretical model for the closed, 4-level Zeeman shifted V-system and demonstrate scalar and vector magnetic fields measurements at video frame rates over spatial dimensions of 5 cm (expandable to $>$ 1 m) with 0.1 mm resolution.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition allows for $\sim\mu$s response time and a large dynamic range ($\mu$T to many Ts).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: A new type of atomic magnetometer using Yb quantum states was discovered in an experiment that employed stimulated optical forces to effectively manipulate atomic velocities and trajectories~\cite{narong2021stimulated .}
elements.sent-2411-007: This Yb magnetometer operates with a single 556 nm laser on the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition (Fig.~\ref{fig:setup ).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: We observe dark stripes in the bright green fluorescence, visible by eye in the presence of magnetic field gradients and multiple strong optical fields.
elements.sent-2411-009: These stripes, which represent contours of constant magnetic fields, enable direct mapping of spatial gradients and non-uniform magnetic fields with rapid response time.
elements.sent-2411-010: The magnetometer can also measure the vector orientation of magnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-011: The observed dark stripes resemble those from Coherent Population Trapping (CPT)~\cite{arimondo_v_1996 , first observed in 1976 by Alzetta et al.~\cite{alzetta1976experimental .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Soon after, Tam \& Happer described imaging inhomogeneous magnetic fields in sodium vapor using optical-RF double resonance~\cite{tam1977optically .}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, the Yb dark stripes result from different physics--primarily Autler-Townes (AT) splitting~\cite{autler_stark_1955 combined with the spatial Hanle effect~\cite{hanle1924magnetische, novikov1975hanle .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: AT splitting in our system is produced by two or more ``strong" laser fields, where strong is relative to the low saturation intensity, $I_{sat = 136$ \muWcm for the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition driven in a V-configuration.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: While AT, CPT, and Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT)~\cite{harris_electromagnetically1997, fleischhauer_electromagnetically_2005 are all quantum phenomena that can lead to decreased absorption and fluorescence in multilevel systems, Abi-Salloum and Anisimov et al. suggested that CPT does not occur in closed V-systems like ours with even isotopes of Yb~\cite{abi-salloum_electromagnetically_2010, anisimov_objectively_2011 , although others came to different conclusions~\cite{vafafard_coherent_2016, rathod_magnetometry_2015, mompart2001coherent, singh2015observation .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Our observations and model best agree with AT.
elements.sent-2411-017: To understand the physics of the Yb dark stripes, we developed a numerical model of the 4-level, closed \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition driven by multiple strong optical fields.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: This model allows direct computation of magnetic field tomography from images collected in experiments as well as generation of fluorescence images given B-field input.
elements.sent-2411-019: The results and analysis are broadly applicable to other atoms with similar structures (e.g. Ca, Sr, Mg).
elements.sent-2411-020: The simple experimental setup in Fig.\ref{fig:setup uses a 556 nm light sheet (3 mm $\times$ 30 mm) with multiple coherent sidebands intersecting a Yb thermal atomic beam.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The fluorescence, captured by a camera, is sensitive to the magnetic field's projection relative to the laser polarization, which is linearly polarized at a rotatable angle $\theta$ to the $z$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-022: Large anti-Helmholtz coils along $z$ create a magnetic field gradient, primarily $dB_z/dz$.
elements.sent-2411-023: The inset image was taken with 2 MHz modulation and $y-$polarized light ($\theta = \pi/2$).
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Figures/Fig1_setup.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:setup}Experimental system: a thermal atomic beam traveling horizontally (+$z$) is intersected by a 556 nm laser ($+x$) in resonance with the \state{1}{S}{0}-\state{3}{P}{1} transition in Yb and amplitude-modulated by an AOM. Anti-Helmholtz coils produce B and $dB_z/dz$ primarily along the $z$ direction. Imaging atomic fluorescence with the camera looking down the $y$-axis reveals a series of dark stripes in the $xz$ plane (10 mm $\times$ 30 mm), visible by eye and a simple camera. The laser is linearly polarized at a rotatable angle $\theta$ to the $z$-axis. }
elements.sent-2411-024: Yb is well-suited for imaging spatially and temporally varying magnetic fields due to compelling characteristics of the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: With an intermediate transition lifetime (875 ns), the linewidth (182 kHz) is narrow enough to provide good magnetic field sensitivity ($\approx$1 nT for a 3 ms image).
elements.sent-2411-026: The low saturation intensity ($I_{sat =136$ \muWcm) allows mW laser powers to generate strong-field AT splitting and high dark-line contrast.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The short lifetime also ensures good spatial resolution (around 0.1 mm longitudinally) and short response times ($\sim\mu$s), enabling recording of fast magnetic field dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-028: The 556 nm green wavelength is ideal for imaging (see SM~\cite{supplement for example videos).}
elements.sent-2411-029: These features distinguish this Yb magnetometer from others, offering new, complementary applications.
elements.sent-2411-030: This system achieves rapid, spatially-resolved B-field tomography over a wide range of fields, from $\mu$T to many Ts.
elements.sent-2411-031: We show later that the magnetic field at each dark stripe can be computed accurately and directly using known g-factors and laser sideband characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-032: Beyond scalar measurements, our Yb magnetometer can measure the B-field vector orientation because both the AT splitting and the spatial Hanle effect are sensitive to the laser polarization E.
elements.sent-2411-033: The observed fluorescence is highly dependent on the relative orientation of E, the local magnetic field vector B, and the observation direction.
elements.sent-2411-034: Some EIT~\cite{cox_measurements_2011, mckelvy2023application and CPT~\cite{pradhan_polarization_2012 vector magnetometers also have this capability.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: As shown later, in some cases, even a single laser beam with a fixed polarization can determine all three components of B from a fluorescence image using forward simulations and parameter space searches based on our model.
elements.sent-2411-036: The market for atomic magnetometers (Cs, Rb, K, He, proton, NV-diamond) is much smaller than that for solid-state devices (Hall, GMR, fluxgates), but atomic magnetometers offer excellent sensitivity and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-037: Many high-performance devices rely on optical pumping or CPT, detected via optical absorption or polarization rotation.
elements.sent-2411-038: Significant advances are summarized in these excellent reviews~\cite{budker_resonant_2002, budker_kimball_2013, grosz_haji-Sheikh_mukhopadhyay_2018, fagaly_superconducting_2006, jensen_magnetometry_2017, budker_optical_2007-1, mitchell2020colloquium, lu2023recent, brajon2023benchmark .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Most relevant for comparative context are magnetometers capable of real-time imaging of magnetic fields (scalar or vector) or rapid tomography~\cite{tam1977optically, castellucci_atomic_2021, vengalattore_high-resolution_2007, yang_scanning_2017, courteille_magnetic_2001, nielsen_characterization_2008, qiu_visualization_2021, lu2023twodimensional, jackson_magneto-optical_2019, lu2023two, hunter2023free, mikhailov2009magnetic, lu2023two, alem2017magnetic .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Some of these systems utilize BECs~\cite{vengalattore_high-resolution_2007, yang_scanning_2017, wildermuth2006sensing or cold atoms~\cite{nielsen2008characterization, eliasson2019spatially to achieve excellent spatial resolution and sensitivity.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Vapor cells enable diverse applications including absorption imaging with vortex laser beams for 3D vector B-field measurements~\cite{castellucci_atomic_2021 , vapor cell gradiometers\cite{sheng2017microfabricated, lucivero2022femtotesla, deans2021electromagnetic, lucivero2022femtotesla, campbell2022gradient , and arrays~\cite{boto2017new, borna2017twenty, coussens2024modular .}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: More similar examples to ours include B-field gradient imaging in Cs vapor cells using optical-microwave double-resonance~\cite{fescenko2014imaging and imaging of dark spots in Sr fluorescence on the singlet \state{1 {S {0 -\state{1 {P {1 transition near zero magnetic field~\cite{jackson_magneto-optical_2019 due to the spatial Hanle effect, but with weak fields and no AT effect.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Avan and Cohen-Tanoudji analyzed the Hanle effect in the 4-level V-system with strong fields~\cite{avan1975hanle .}
elements.sent-2411-044: We adapted the analysis in~\cite{jackson_magneto-optical_2019 to incorporate the Hanle effect in our model.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Model-- We consider the closed, 4-level \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition (Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (a)) in even isotopes (most abundant \textsuperscript{174 Yb) with zero nuclear spin ($I=0$) and no hyperfine structure or complications of optical pumping.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The \state{3 {P {1 state consists of Zeeman states $m_J=-1,0,1$, with corresponding energy shifts $m_J g \mu_B |B|$ (in the low field regime), with $g=1.49282$~\cite{martin1978atomic and $\mu_B$ the Bohr magneton.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: This transition is driven by amplitude-modulated light at modulation frequency $\delta_{mod}$, which consists of a carrier at $\omega_0$ (set to the resonance frequency of the $m_J=0$ state) plus a series of evenly spaced sidebands at $\omega_0 \pm n\delta_{mod}$, where $n = 1, 2, 3,...$ We initially include only the two first-harmonic sidebands at frequencies $\omega_0 \pm \delta_{mod}$ of the laser polarized along $y$ ($\theta = \pi/2$) perpendicular to the imaging plane, with the quantization axis along $z$ and no magnetic field along $x$ and $y$ directions (Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-048: The $y-$polarized light can be decomposed into a combination of LCP and RCP light that drive $\sigma^+$ and $\sigma^-$ transitions to the $m_J = \pm1$ states, forming a Zeeman-shifted V-system.
elements.sent-2411-049: With symmetric detunings and Zeeman shifts, resonances occur at $g\mu_B|B| = \hbar\delta_{mod}$.
elements.sent-2411-050: In this configuration, strong-field AC Stark effects cause the three coupled states to split (AT splitting)~\cite{autler_stark_1955 , which leads to decreased absorption and fluorescence on resonance.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{Figures/Fig2_model.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence}(a) Energy levels of the Yb \state{1}{S}{0}-\state{3}{P}{1} transition showing Autler-Townes splitting in three states when driven by strong resonant fields with equal Rabi frequency $\Omega$. Our initial model includes only the first harmonic sidebands at $\omega_0 \pm \delta_{mod}$ and B-fields along $z$. In this setting, $y$-polarized fields produce $\sigma^\pm$ transitions to $m_J=\pm1$ states, and resonances occur at $b_z = \pm \delta_{mod}$. (b) Autler-Townes splitting caused a decrease in fluorescence ($\propto$ total excited-state population $\rho_e$) on resonance when using strong driving fields ($\Omega = 15\gamma$) but not with weak fields ($\Omega = \gamma$). (c) With strong fields, Doppler-broadened fluorescence has three minima at $b_z = 0$ and $\pm \delta_{mod}$. With the spatial Hanle effect included (bottom curve), the fluorescence emitted along $y$ goes to zero where $b_z=0$. The error bands are one standard deviation. }
elements.sent-2411-051: For laser polarization $\theta$ and arbitrary B$=(B_x, B_y, B_z)$, the Hamiltonian of this 4-level atom is \begin{equation}\label{H_theta_xyz} H_\theta= \hbar \begin{pmatrix} 0 \Omega_{\sigma}^*(t)\sin{\theta} \Omega_{\pi}^*(t)\cos{\theta} \Omega_{\sigma}^*(t)\sin{\theta} \Omega_{\sigma}(t)\sin{\theta} \omega_0 + b_z b_{xy}^* 0 \Omega_{\pi}(t)\cos{\theta} b_{xy} \omega_0 b_{xy}^* \Omega_{\sigma}(t)\sin{\theta} 0 b_{xy}^* \omega_0 - b_z \end{pmatrix}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{H_theta_xyz} H_\theta= \hbar  0 \Omega_{\sigma}^*(t)\sin{\theta} \Omega_{\pi}^*(t)\cos{\theta} \Omega_{\sigma}^*(t)\sin{\theta} \Omega_{\sigma}(t)\sin{\theta} \omega_0 + b_z b_{xy}^* 0 \Omega_{\pi}(t)\cos{\theta} b_{xy} \omega_0 b_{xy}^* \Omega_{\sigma}(t)\sin{\theta} 0 b_{xy}^* \omega_0 - b_z .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-052: Here $b_z = g\mu_B B_z/\hbar$ denotes the Zeeman (frequency) shift due to the $B_z$ field component.
elements.sent-2411-053: Similarly, $b_{xy}$ encapsulates the remaining magnetic field components $B_x$ and $B_y$ where $b_{xy = \frac{g\mu_B {\hbar\sqrt{2 (B_x + iB_y)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: Strong electric dipole interactions produce the time-dependent matrix elements of $H_{\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The pre-factors $i/\sqrt{6}$ and $1/\sqrt{3}$ are Clebsch-Gordan coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-056: With the Hamiltonian, we calculate the total time-averaged fluorescence by summing the excited-state population from the density matrix $\rho$, which is obtained by numerically solving the Lindblad Master Equation using the Python package QuTiP~\cite{johanssonQuTiPOpensourcePython2012, johanssonQuTiPPythonFramework2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The code is available on GitHub~\cite{nanarong2024githubYbmag with a documentation provided in TN's thesis~\cite{narong2023thesis .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In the calculation we make the rotating-wave approximation as the modulation frequencies (100 kHz-10 MHz) and weak-field Zeeman shifts ($\approx$2.1 MHz/G = 21 kHz/$\mu$T ) are small relative to $\omega_0$.
elements.sent-2411-059: In the simplest case where the magnetic field is along $z$ ($b_{xy =0$) and $\theta=\pi/2$, only $\sigma$ transitions are allowed.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Assuming equal Rabi frequencies ($\Omega_1 = \Omega_2 = \Omega$) and with $\delta_{mod =15\gamma$, Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (b) shows the total excited-state population $\rho_e$ as a function of Zeeman shift $b_z$ for weak and strong fields.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: On the resonances at $b_z = \pm \delta_{mod}$ where the Zeeman shifts match the field detunings, only the strong fields ($\Omega = 15\gamma$) produced apparent Autler-Townes splitting and a decrease in fluorescence on the resonances.
elements.sent-2411-062: These fluorescence ``dips" remain visible in the presence of Doppler broadening due to atomic velocities $v_x$ in the direction of laser propagation (Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-063: We included this effect by averaging $\rho_e$ across $N=1000$ transverse velocities $v_x$ sampled from a Gaussian distribution with zero mean and standard deviation of 4.5 m/s (FWHM $\approx$10.6 m/s) estimated from the atomic beam divergence.
elements.sent-2411-064: The Doppler broadened curve has three minima at $b_z = 0, \pm \delta_{mod}$, which would result in three dark stripes on a fluorescence image.
elements.sent-2411-065: At $\Omega = 15\gamma$, the FWHM of the dips is around $ 5\gamma \approx 900$ kHz, which is significantly narrower than the Doppler width (19 MHz).
elements.sent-2411-066: While the computed total fluorescence accurately predicts the location of the dark lines, $\rho_e$ does not account for the system geometry, where the camera only collects fluorescence emitted within in a cone around the $+y$ direction.
elements.sent-2411-067: To improve predictions of lineshapes and contrast, we incorporated the spatial Hanle effect to account for the spatial distribution of the dipole radiation.
elements.sent-2411-068: This effect was carefully studied by Jackson \& Durfee for the broad \state{1 {S {0 -\state{1 {P {1 transition in Sr driven by weak fields~\cite{jackson_magneto-optical_2019 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: We adapted their analysis to compute the fluorescence emitted in the $y$-direction ($I_y$) from the density matrix (see SM~\cite{supplement for the derivation), resulting in the bottom curve in Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (c).}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The Hanle effect did not alter the locations of the dark lines but the signal magnitude roughly halved, and $I_y$ went to zero at $b_z = 0$, as expected for $y$-polarized electric dipole moments.
elements.sent-2411-071: This correction improved agreement with our experimental results, but some discrepancies remained.
elements.sent-2411-072: Next, we generalize the dark-resonance conditions for more than two driving fields and for 3D magnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-073: First, we note that, with Doppler broadening, any two sidebands in the square wave harmonics (Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (a)) produce dark resonances when their relative detuning (an integer multiple of $\delta_{mod}$ matches the Zeeman shifts between the $m_J = \pm1$ states ($2g\mu_B|B|$).}
elements.sent-2411-074: Hence, the dark-resonance condition becomes \begin{equation}\label{eq:dark-line-condition} \frac{g\mu_B}{\hbar}|B| = \frac{n}{2}\delta_{mod},\end{equation} where $n= 1, 2, 3, ...$.
elements.sent-2411-075: Equation~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition now describes a series of contours of constant magnetic fields where the atomic fluorescence goes ``dark" and enables direct computation of $|B|$ at the location of each dark stripe based on the known $\delta_{mod}$ and the g-factor.}
elements.sent-2411-076: To verify this relationship and better reproduce the experimental results, we extended the model to include 7 strong fields in total: the carrier and evenly spaced sidebands up to the third harmonics.
elements.sent-2411-077: We then analyze the dark resonances explicitly in the 3D picture, by expressing $|B|$ in terms of its vector components and exploring the parameter space $(b_x, b_y, b_z) = g\mu_BB/\hbar$ to investigate the effects of introducing $x$- and $y$-components of the B-fields on the observed fluorescence.
elements.sent-2411-078: To illustrate, we computed the fluorescence $I_y$ as a function of uniformly varying $(b_y, b_z)$ , while keeping $b_x=0$.
elements.sent-2411-079: With $y$-polarized light and the carrier and higher harmonics included, Fig.~\ref{fig:contour (a) shows dark concentric ``circles" with radii $n\delta_{mod /2 = 10\gamma, 20\gamma, ...$, in agreement with \eqref{eq:dark-line-condition , and $I_y$ at $b_z=0$ remains zero due to the Hanle effect.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Horizontal cross-sections in Fig.~\ref{fig:contour (b) at constant $b_y=0, 5\gamma, 10\gamma$ show apparent shifts in the locations of the dips on the line scans.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Similar plots for $(b_x, b_z)$ are given in the SM~\cite{supplement .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Unlike the $y$ component, introducing $B_x$ perpendicular to the laser polarization causes the dipole precession about the $x$-axis, allowing some emission along $y$ and lifting the fluorescence minimum at $B_z=0$ from zero.
elements.sent-2411-083: This is a distinct effect that cannot be produced by $B_y$ alone.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{Figures/Fig3_model_contour.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:contour}(a) Computed fluorescence $I_y$ as a function of ($b_y$, $b_z$) showing dark resonances as concentric ``rings" with radii $n\delta_{mod}/2$ ($\theta=\pi/2$, carrier and higher-harmonic sidebands included). Note this is not the same as the spatial image. (b) Horizontal cross-sections at constant $b_y=0, 5\gamma, 10\gamma$ showing apparent shifts in the fluorescence dips. }
elements.sent-2411-084: Experiment-- For small fields (less than 200 $\mu$T), our model enables us to determine $|B_x|$, $|B_y|$ and $|B_z|$ with a single laser polarization.
elements.sent-2411-085: Starting with a fluorescence image in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (a) with a series of vertical dark stripes resulting primarily from a $B_z$ field with a $dB_z/dz$ gradient generated by the large coils, we extracted the dark-line locations and computed the field magnitude $|B|$ with~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition , plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (b) with a quadratic least-square fit.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: According to the Hanle effect, the darkest spot near $z$=$-5$ mm indicates where $B_z = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The laser was square-wave AM modulated at 2 MHz at 10\% duty cycle and $y$-polarized.
elements.sent-2411-088: The thin ``sheet" of light in the $xz$-plane does not capture variations along the $y$-axis (none expected on that scale).
elements.sent-2411-089: Additional data points were extracted from images taken with 1 MHz and 4 MHz modulation.
elements.sent-2411-090: From these three frames (3 ms), we estimate the uncertainty in $|B|$ to be $\approx$ 1 $\mu$T (0.01 G) based on the width of the dark lines and signal-to-noise ratio.
elements.sent-2411-091: This precision can be improved with more frames.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Figures/Fig4_expt.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:B-field-mapping}B-field tomography from (a) the fluorescence image with dark stripes that correspond to contours of constant magnetic fields. The darkest spot near $z = -5$ mm is where $B_z = 0$. The laser beam was $y$-polarized and modulated at 2 MHz. The dashed line marks where the line-scan in (c) was taken. (b) $|B|$ calculated at the dark-stripe locations extracted from (a) and additional frames taken at 1 and 4 MHz (error bars too small to show). $|B_z|$ (dashed line) was calculated based on the estimates $B_x=0.12$ G, $B_y=0.36$ G, determined by fitting the model to the line-scan in (c). Both curves are quadratic least square fits. (c) Intensity line-scan taken from (a) and theoretically calculated fluorescence assuming $B_x = B_y = 0$ (green dotted line) and with $B_x = 0.12$ G, $B_y = 0.36$ G (orange dashed line), assumed uniform across $z$. Each uncertainty band represents the standard deviation.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Next, we investigate the agreement between our theoretical fluorescence and experiment.
elements.sent-2411-093: Using the computed $|B|$, we first generated a theoretical fluorescence curve by assuming $B_x$=$B_y$=0 and that the B-field gradient was solely $dB_z/dz$ (green curve).
elements.sent-2411-094: This baseline calculation produced the green dotted curve in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (c) with the uncertainty band (one standard deviation).}
elements.sent-2411-095: We see two significant discrepancies from the data (black curve).
elements.sent-2411-096: First, the minimum intensity near $z=-5$ mm is well above zero, indicating non-zero $B_x$ as discussed earlier.
elements.sent-2411-097: Second, apparent shifts in the dips suggest that $B_y \neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-098: Using our model and interactively calculating the fluorescence for a range of constant $B_x, B_y$ values we found that $B_x=0.12(2)$ G, $B_y=0.36(2)$ G produced the theoretical curve (orange dashed line) that best agrees with the experiment (black curve).
elements.sent-2411-099: These $B_x, B_y$ values are consistent with independent Hall probe measurements of the Earth's field outside the vacuum chamber, and within uncertainties of the Hall sensor.
elements.sent-2411-100: With these estimates, we computed revised values of $|B_z|$, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (b).}
elements.sent-2411-101: It was initially surprising that a single sheet of $y$-polarized light in the $xz$-plane provides information about both the field magnitude and direction including variations of $B_x$ and $B_z$ in the $xz$-plane (assuming no significant variation of B in the thin (3 mm) $y$-direction).
elements.sent-2411-102: The additional vector information comes from the spatial Hanle effect at small fields, where fluorescence redistributes due to the magnetic moment's precession in local fields increasing away from B=0~\cite{jackson_magneto-optical_2019, narong2023thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-103: A detailed exploration of these vector capabilities is left for future work.
elements.sent-2411-104: Most atomic magnetometers (Rb, Cs, K, He) are small vapor cells (mm-cm), with high atomic densities (10\textsuperscript{10 -10\textsuperscript{12 /cm\textsuperscript{3 ) that measure uniform fields by optical absorption or polarization rotation~\cite{budker_kimball_2013, grosz_haji-Sheikh_mukhopadhyay_2018, fagaly_superconducting_2006, jensen_magnetometry_2017, budker_optical_2007-1, mitchell2020colloquium, lu2023recent .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: In contrast, this Yb magnetometer relies on Autler-Townes splitting for all magnetic field magnitudes and the spatial-Hanle effect at low fields.
elements.sent-2411-106: This combination enables exciting imaging and vector capabilities as demonstrated, with measurement bandwidths from DC to $\approx$500 kHz and no dead-time.
elements.sent-2411-107: It can measure fields from $\mu T$ to $>$10 T, though we have not tested beyond 20 mT (200 G).
elements.sent-2411-108: The atomic density (10\textsuperscript{9 /cm\textsuperscript{3 , or $\sim$10\textsuperscript{10 /cm\textsuperscript{3 when including other isotopes and Doppler broadening) is low enough to avoid collision limitations, and optical pumping does not occur in even isotopes of Yb.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Our current experimental system effectively demonstrates the concepts and capabilities of the Yb quantum imaging magnetometer, but it is not designed, nor optimal, for magnetometry.
elements.sent-2411-110: Yb has several unique capabilities and advantages, but also limitations.
elements.sent-2411-111: It requires high temperatures ($\gtrapprox$400 $^\circ$C) due to its low vapor pressure, making vapor cell production challenging.
elements.sent-2411-112: The ultimate sensitivity is also lower than typical atomic magnetometers due to the broader linewidth (182 kHz vs $\approx$0.1-1 kHz) and lower atomic density.
elements.sent-2411-113: Additionally, at higher fields, signals from other Yb isotopes may need to be addressed.
elements.sent-2411-114: Nonetheless, our current system produces bright green fluorescence images in 3 ms (camera frame rate limited), with $>$10,000 spatially-resolved B-field measurements.
elements.sent-2411-115: The estimated sensitivity is $\approx$1 nT for a 3 ms image, with a field uncertainty of $\approx$100 nT~\cite{narong2023thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-116: This approach does not require a well-collimated atomic beam and works best with Doppler averaging.
elements.sent-2411-117: Fluorescence is detected from all atoms within the homogeneous excitation width, including modulation sidebands.
elements.sent-2411-118: Power-broadened linewidths of the dark stripes ranged from $\approx$ 360 kHz to 2 MHz (FWHM), much narrower than the $\approx$19 MHz transverse Doppler width (see SM~\cite{supplement ).}
elements.sent-2411-119: The system operates effectively in an unshielded environment and opens up possibilities for new applications complementary to existing magnetic sensors, covering a wider range of spatial, temporal, and field parameters.
elements.sent-2411-120: We are grateful to many who have contributed to our understanding of this Yb magnetometer, including Stuart Ingleby, Erling Riis, and the B-sensing group at Strathclyde University, Svenja Knappe, John Kitching, Benjamin Lev, Sonja Frank-Arnold, Jason Hogan, and Megan Nantel.

elements.sent-2411-127: We appreciate valuable assistance with the experiments by summer students: Luz Elena Martinez, Daniel Kuelbs, Judy Liu, and Zhengruilong Wang.. \nocite{bai1986transient.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We show good agreement between experimental data and our theoretical model for the closed, 4-level Zeeman shifted V-system and demonstrate scalar and vector magnetic field measurements at video frame rates over spatial dimensions of 5 cm with 0.1 mm resolution.
elements.sent-2411-129: Additionally, the \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition allows for $\sim\mu$s response time and a large dynamic range (from microtesla to many tesla).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-130: A new type of atomic magnetometer that uses Yb quantum states was discovered in an experiment that employed stimulated optical forces to effectively manipulate atomic velocities and trajectories~\cite{narong2021stimulated .}
elements.sent-2411-131: We observe dark stripes in bright green fluorescence, visible by eye in the presence of magnetic field gradients and multiple strong optical fields.
elements.sent-2411-132: These stripes, which represent contours of constant magnetic field, enable direct mapping of spatial gradients and nonuniform magnetic fields with rapid response time.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{Fig1_setup.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:setup}Experimental system: a thermal atomic beam traveling horizontally (+$z$) is intersected by a 556 nm laser ($+x$) in resonance with the \state{1}{S}{0}-\state{3}{P}{1} transition in Yb and amplitude-modulated by an AOM. Anti-Helmholtz coils produce B and $dB_z/dz$ primarily along the $z$ direction. Imaging atomic fluorescence with the camera looking down the $y$-axis reveals a series of dark stripes in the $xz$ plane (10 mm $\times$ 30 mm), visible by eye and a simple camera. The laser is linearly polarized at a rotatable angle $\theta$ to the $z$-axis. }
elements.sent-2411-133: The dark stripes observed resemble those from Coherent Population Trapping (CPT)~\cite{arimondo_v_1996 , first observed in 1976 by Alzetta et al.~\cite{alzetta1976experimental .}}
elements.sent-2411-134: However, the Yb dark stripes result from different physics----mainly Autler-Townes (AT) splitting~\cite{autler_stark_1955 combined with the spatial Hanle effect~\cite{hanle1924magnetische, novikov1975hanle .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Although AT, CPT, and Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT)~\cite{harris_electromagnetically1997, fleischhauer_electromagnetically_2005 are all quantum phenomena that can lead to decreased absorption and fluorescence in multilevel systems, refs.~\cite{abi-salloum_electromagnetically_2010, anisimov_objectively_2011, zhou2019analysis show that CPT and EIT do not occur in closed V systems that have ground states with zero electronic and nuclear spin, such as even isotopes of Yb.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Other papers treat different configurations and come to different conclusions~\cite{vafafard_coherent_2016, rathod_magnetometry_2015, mompart2001coherent, singh2015observation .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Our observations and the model agree best with AT as the dominant mechanism (see SM~\cite{supplement ).}
elements.sent-2411-138: To understand the physics of the dark stripes, we developed a numerical model of the 4-level, closed \state{1 {S {0 -\state{3 {P {1 transition driven by multiple strong optical fields.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: The low $I_{sat}$ allows mW laser powers to generate strong-field AT splitting and high dark-line contrast.
elements.sent-2411-140: The short lifetime also ensures good spatial resolution (around 0.1 mm longitudinally) and enables recording of fast ($\sim\mu$s) magnetic field dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-141: These features distinguish this Yb magnetometer from others, offering new and complementary applications.
elements.sent-2411-142: Vapor cells enable diverse applications, including absorption imaging with vortex laser beams for 3D vector measurements~\cite{castellucci_atomic_2021 , gradiometers~\cite{sheng2017microfabricated, lucivero2022femtotesla, deans2021electromagnetic, lucivero2022femtotesla, campbell2022gradient , and arrays~\cite{boto2017new, borna2017twenty, coussens2024modular .}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: We adapted their calculation of directional fluorescence in our model.
elements.sent-2411-144: The \state{3 {P {1 state consists of Zeeman states $m_J=-1,0,1$, with corresponding energy shifts $m_J g \mu_B |B|$ (in the low field regime), with $g=1.49282(5)$~\cite{martin1978atomic, budick1967gj, baumann1968gj and $\mu_B$ the Bohr magneton.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: In our setup, this transition is driven by amplitude-modulated light at modulation frequency $\delta_{mod}$, which consists of a carrier at $\omega_0$ (set to the resonance frequency of the $m_J=0$ state) plus a series of evenly spaced sidebands at $\omega_0 \pm n\delta_{mod}$, where $n = 1, 2, 3,...$ The model initially included only the two first-harmonic sidebands at $\omega_0 \pm \delta_{mod}$ of the laser polarized along $y$ ($\theta = \pi/2$) perpendicular to the imaging plane, with the quantization axis along $z$ and no magnetic field along $x$ and $y$ directions (Fig.~\ref{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence (a)).}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{Fig2_model.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:levels_AT_fluorescence}(a) Energy levels of the Yb \state{1}{S}{0}-\state{3}{P}{1} transition showing AT splitting in three states when driven by strong resonant fields with equal Rabi frequency $\Omega$. Our initial model includes only the first harmonic sidebands at $\omega_0 \pm \delta_{mod}$ and B-fields along $z$. $y$-polarized fields drive $\sigma^\pm$ transitions to $m_J=\pm1$ states, and resonances occur at $b_z = \pm \delta_{mod}$. (b) AT splitting caused a decrease in fluorescence ($\propto$ total excited-state population $\rho_e$) on resonance with strong driving fields ($\Omega = 15\gamma$) but not with weak fields ($\Omega = \gamma$). (c) With strong fields, Doppler-broadened fluorescence has three minima at $b_z = 0$ and $\pm \delta_{mod}$. With the spatial Hanle effect included (bottom curve), the fluorescence emitted along $y$ goes to zero where $b_z=0$. Uncertainty bands represent one standard deviation for Monte Carlo simulations with $N$ atoms. }
elements.sent-2411-146: Here, $b_z = g\mu_B B_z/\hbar$ denotes the Zeeman (frequency) shift due to the $B_z$ field component.
elements.sent-2411-147: In the calculation we make the rotating-wave approximation as the modulation frequencies (100 kHz-10 MHz) and weak-field Zeeman shifts ($\approx$2.1 MHz/G = 21 kHz/$\mu$T) are small relative to $\omega_0$.
elements.sent-2411-148: The uncertainty band shows the standard deviation $\sqrt{\bar\rho_e(1-\bar\rho_e)/N}$ for a Monte Carlo simulation with $N$ atoms.
elements.sent-2411-149: Equation~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition now describes a series of contours of constant magnetic fields where the fluorescence goes ``dark" and enables direct computation of $|B|$ at the location of each dark stripe based on the known $\delta_{mod}$ and the g-factor.}
elements.sent-2411-150: We also analyzed the dark resonances explicitly in the 3D picture, by expressing $|B|$ in terms of its vector components and investigating the effects of $B_x$ and $B_y$ on the fluorescence pattern (see End Matter).
elements.sent-2411-151: Forward modeling of $I_y$ enabled us to estimate all components $|B_x|$, $|B_y|$, and $|B_z|$ at small fields (below 200 $\mu$T) from experimental data as shown below.
elements.sent-2411-152: Experiment-- Starting with a fluorescence image in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (a) with a series of vertical dark stripes resulting primarily from a $B_z$ field with a $dB_z/dz$ gradient generated by the large coils, we extracted the dark-line locations and computed the field magnitude $|B|$ with Eq.~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition , plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping (b) with a quadratic least-square fit.}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: The thin ``sheet" of light in the $xz$-plane does not capture variations in B along the $y$-axis (none expected on that scale).
elements.sent-2411-154: This assumption could break down if variations of B in the $y$-direction over the sheet's thickness $\Delta y$ is greater than the power-broadened transition linewidth, in which case it would blur out the dark lines.
elements.sent-2411-155: In our setup, $\Delta y=3$ mm and the power-broadened (dark) linewidths range from 360 kHz to 2 MHz, corresponding to $\Delta B\approx$ 7-100 $\mu$T (70 mG to 1 G) taking into account both $\Delta m_J = 1,2$ V-transitions.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{Fig3_expt.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:B-field-mapping}B-field tomography from (a) the fluorescence image with dark stripes that correspond to contours of constant magnetic fields. The darkest spot near $z = -5$ mm is where $B_z = 0$. The laser beam was $y$-polarized and modulated at 2 MHz. The dashed line marks where the line-scan in (c) was taken. (b) $|B|$ calculated at the dark-stripe locations extracted from (a) and additional frames taken at 1 and 4 MHz (error bars too small to show). $|B_z|$ (dashed line) was calculated based on the estimates $B_x=0.12$ G, $B_y=0.36$ G, determined by fitting the model to the line-scan in (c). Both curves are quadratic least square fits. (c) Intensity line-scan taken from (a) and theoretically calculated fluorescence assuming $B_x = B_y = 0$ (green dotted line) and with $B_x = 0.12$ G, $B_y = 0.36$ G (orange dashed line), assumed uniform across $z$. Each uncertainty band represents the standard deviation.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We see two significant discrepancies from the experiment (black curve).
elements.sent-2411-157: First, the minimum fluorescence near $z=-5$ mm (where $B_z=0$) is well above zero even after background subtraction.
elements.sent-2411-158: This indicates that $B_x\neq0$ because $B_x$ causes dipole precession about the $x$-axis (away from the polarization axis $y$), allowing some fluorescence to reach the camera.
elements.sent-2411-159: Second, the apparent shifts and distortion near the first dip suggest that $B_y \neq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-160: With $y-$polarized light, $B_y$ effectively shifts the locations of the dark lines (contours of constant $|B|$) without lifting the fluorescence minimum from zero because $B_y$ does not change the dipole orientation, an important distinction from $B_x$ (see End Matter for more details and illustrations in Figs.~\ref{fig:contour_bxbz and~\ref{fig:contour_bybz .) Using our model and interactively calculating the fluorescence (see End Matter) for a range of constant $B_x, B_y$ values, we found that $|B_x|=0.12(2)$ G, $|B_y=|0.36(2)$ G produced the theoretical curve (orange dashed line) that best agrees with the experiment (black curve).}}
elements.sent-2411-161: These values are consistent with independent Hall probe measurements of the Earth's field outside the vacuum chamber, and within uncertainties of the Hall sensor.
elements.sent-2411-162: Discussion-- It was initially surprising that a single sheet of $y$-polarized light in the $xz$-plane provides information about both the field magnitude and direction, including variations of $B_x$ and $B_z$ in the $xz$-plane (assuming no significant variation of B in the thin (3 mm) $y$-direction).
elements.sent-2411-163: Most atomic magnetometers (Rb, Cs, K, He) use small vapor cells (mm-cm), with high atomic densities (10\textsuperscript{10 -10\textsuperscript{12 /cm\textsuperscript{3 ) that measure uniform fields via optical absorption or polarization rotation~\cite{budker_kimball_2013, grosz_haji-Sheikh_mukhopadhyay_2018, fagaly_superconducting_2006, jensen_magnetometry_2017, budker_optical_2007-1, mitchell2020colloquium, lu2023recent .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: It can measure fields from $\mu T$ to $>$10 T (see SM~\cite{supplement ), though we have not tested beyond 20 mT (200 G).}
elements.sent-2411-165: The atomic density (up to 10\textsuperscript{10 /cm\textsuperscript{3 ) is low enough to avoid collision limitations, and optical pumping does not occur in even isotopes.}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Our current setup effectively demonstrates the concepts and capabilities of the Yb quantum imaging magnetometer, but it is not designed, nor optimal, for magnetometry.
elements.sent-2411-167: Yb has several unique capabilities, but also limitations.
elements.sent-2411-168: The ultimate sensitivity is lower than typical atomic magnetometers due to the broader linewidth (182 kHz vs $\approx$0.1-1 kHz) and lower atomic density.
elements.sent-2411-169: However, our current system produces bright green fluorescence images in 3 ms (camera frame rate limited), with $>$ 10,000 spatially resolved B-field measurements over the 10$\times$30 mm\textsuperscript{2 interaction region.}
elements.sent-2411-170: Using five cameras, a spatial range of 1 m with 0.1 mm resolution would be feasible (see SM~\cite{supplement ).}
elements.sent-2411-171: Our approach does not require a well-collimated atomic beam and works best with Doppler averaging.
elements.sent-2411-172: Power-broadened linewidths of the dark stripes ranged from 360 kHz to 2 MHz (FWHM), much narrower than the $\approx$19 MHz transverse Doppler width.
elements.sent-2411-173: This Yb system operates effectively in an unshielded environment and opens up possibilities for new applications complementary to existing magnetic sensors, covering a wider range of spatial, temporal, and field parameters.
elements.sent-2411-174: We are grateful to many who have contributed to our understanding of this Yb magnetometer, including Stuart Ingleby, Erling Riis, and the B-sensing group at Strathclyde University, Svenja Knappe, John Kitching, Benjamin Lev, Sonja Franke-Arnold, Jason Hogan, and Megan Nantel.

elements.sent-2411-180: Appendix: $B_x$ and $B_y$ field components alter the observed fluorescence differently-- Our model enables direct computation of fluorescence as a function of an arbitrary magnetic field $B = (B_x, B_y, B_z)$.
elements.sent-2411-181: As we generalize the dark resonance condition~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition from the 1D ($B_z$-only) to the full 3D picture, fluorescence contour plots as a function of two varying components of B are useful tools for visualizing the effects of different field components.}
elements.sent-2411-182: As discussed in the letter, due to the spatial Hanle effect, $B_x$ field causes dipole precession away from the polarization axis ($y$, vertical).
elements.sent-2411-183: This net "tilt" from non-zero $B_x$ allows emitted fluorescence to reach the camera looking down vertically, lifting the fluorescence minimum from zero.
elements.sent-2411-184: To quantify and visualize this effect, we plotted the vertical fluorescence $I_y$ as a function of $(b_x, b_z) = \frac{g\mu {\hbar (B_x, B_z)$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:contour_bxbz .}}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Here we assume $b_y=0$ and carefully note that the contour plot shows the variation of the fluorescence in the B-field space.
elements.sent-2411-186: It is not the same a spatial image.
elements.sent-2411-187: The contour plot contains a series of dark concentric rings with radii of $n\delta_{mod /2$ (multiples of $\delta_{mod /2$), in agreement with the dark-resonance conditions defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:dark-line-condition .}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: The horizontal cross-sections at $b_x = 0, 5\gamma, 10\gamma$ show that the minimum fluorescence is no longer zero when $b_x > 0$.
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{EndMatter_Fig4_bxbz.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:contour_bxbz}(a) Computed fluorescence $I_y$ as a function of ($b_x$, $b_z$) showing dark resonances as concentric ``rings" with radii $n\delta_{mod}/2$ ($\theta=\pi/2$, carrier and higher-harmonic sidebands included). Note this is not the same as the spatial image. (b) Horizontal cross-sections at constant $b_x=0, 5\gamma, 10\gamma$ showing reduced contrast at $b_z=0$ due to precession of the atomic dipoles. }
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.49\textwidth]{EndMatter_Fig5_bybz.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:contour_bybz}(a) Computed fluorescence $I_y$ as a function of ($b_y$, $b_z$) showing dark resonances as concentric ``rings" with radii $n\delta_{mod}/2$ ($\theta=\pi/2$, carrier and higher-harmonic sidebands included). Fluorescence is zero at $b_z=0$ regardless of $b_y$ values due to the Hanle effect, but there is apparent broadening of the central minimum. Note this is not the same as the spatial image. (b) Horizontal cross-sections at constant $b_y=0, 5\gamma, 10\gamma$ showing apparent shifts in the fluorescence dips. }
elements.sent-2411-189: On the other hand, we made respective plots for $(b_y, b_z)$ variation in Fig.~\ref{fig:contour_bybz (assuming $b_x=0$ in this case).}
elements.sent-2411-190: The dark-resonance rings are similarly visible, but the main difference here is that the fluorescence $I_y$ remains zero at $b_z=0$ regardless of $b_y$ values, as confirmed by both the contour plot and the cross-sections.
elements.sent-2411-191: This is as expected from the spatial Hanle effect because the $B_y$ field is parallel to the dipole orientation when driven by the $y$-polarized light.
elements.sent-2411-192: The main effects of introducing non-zero $b_y$, as illustrated by the cross-section plots, are broadening of the central minimum and apparent shifts in the other dark-line locations due to the curvature of the dark-resonance rings.
elements.sent-2411-193: Both Figs.~\ref{fig:contour_bxbz and~\ref{fig:contour_bybz show that in our system, the effects of $B_x$ and $B_y$ are discernible at least when the Yb atoms are driven by $y$-polarized light.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: These distinguishable effects on fluorescence allowed us to estimate $|B_x|$ and $|B_y|$ magnitudes from the experimental data, as demonstrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:B-field-mapping .}
elements.sent-2411-195: We first infer the plausible range of $|B_x|$ from the reduced contrast (Fig.~\ref{fig:contour_bxbz ) and that of $|B_y|$ from the broadening of the $b_z=0$ minimum (Fig.~\ref{fig:contour_bxbz ).}}
elements.sent-2411-196: For all pairs of $(|B_x|, |B_y|)$ in the inferred range, we computed theoretical fluorescence curves and determined the $(|B_x|, |B_y|)$ values that produced the best agreement with experimental data.
elements.sent-2411-197: This is an iterative grid search, in which we compare the number and locations of the fluorescence minima, narrowed down the $(|B_x|, |B_y|)$ range, and recomputed the theoretical fluorescence on a finer grid until we achieved a reasonable fit to the experimental fluorescence data.
elements.sent-2411-198: This process enables the vector capabilities of our magnetometer.
